channel.title: Contact | Arenar Intel
channel.description: The Global Intelligence Feed - delivering fast, verified reporting on the events, conflicts and decisions shaping the modern world.

item.title: Neymar to miss Brazil's World Cup match with Haiti
item.dc:creator: The Intel Desk
item.description: Brazil forward Neymar will not travel with the squad for Friday's World Cup match against Haiti as he recovers from a calf injury, the country's football body says.
item.content:encoded:
Neymar will not travel with the Brazil squad to Philadelphia for their second World Cup group game against Haiti as he recovers from a calf injury, the Brazilian Football Association said.

The association said on Thursday that Neymar will remain in New Jersey to complete the final stage of his recovery, using the facilities at The Ridge hotel and the Columbia Park training centre.

Neymar was seen training with the squad on Wednesday after warming up separately, having previously been limited to indoor work with physiotherapists and fitness coaches as Brazil took a cautious approach to his return. He injured his right calf during a 3-0 defeat for his club Santos against Coritiba last month.

The five-time World Cup champions began their campaign with a 1-1 draw against Morocco on Saturday and have one point. Meanwhile, Haiti are bottom of the group after losing to Scotland.

item.title: Broos tells critics to 'shut up' after Mexico loss
item.dc:creator: The Intel Desk
item.description: South Africa coach Hugo Broos has hit back at critics of his tactics after the team's 2-0 loss to Mexico, ahead of Thursday's World Cup clash with the Czech Republic.
item.content:encoded:
Broos tells critics to ‘shut up’ after Mexico loss South Africa coach Hugo Broos has told critics to “shut up” and insisted he will keep doing things his way, after his defensive tactics were blamed for the team’s 2-0 loss to Mexico in their World Cup opener. He made the comments at a press conference ahead of Thursday’s match against the Czech Republic.

The defeat came after a flat performance in which South Africa created few clear chances and finished with nine men, two players having been sent off in the second half.

Broos was defiant at Wednesday’s press conference, telling his critics to “shut up.” He said he had coached for 40 years and accepted that criticism came with the job, but would keep managing the team his own way. “I do it my way,” he said.

He added that South Africa must beat the Czech Republic on Thursday to keep alive their hopes of advancing from Group A, having lost their opener. He hinted at a more attacking approach, saying the team could only improve “if we have the ball.”

item.title: Top US general holds rare meeting with Cuban military officials
item.dc:creator: The Intel Desk
item.description: The commander of US Southern Command met Cuban military officials near Guantanamo Bay in a rare high level military engagement between the two countries.
item.content:encoded:
The head of US Southern Command, General Francis Donovan, held a rare meeting with senior Cuban military officials near the perimeter of Guantanamo Bay on Friday.

According to US military officials, the discussions focused on operational security, force protection and safety issues related to the naval base.

Donovan also conducted a security assessment of the base and met with personnel to review operational readiness and protection measures.

The meeting included Cuban General Roberto Legra Sotolongo, a senior military official and deputy chief of Cuba’s General Staff.

The engagement is notable because direct military contacts between the United States and Cuba remain uncommon despite the countries maintaining communication channels on specific security matters.

The meeting comes during a period of heightened tensions between Washington and Havana following recent US actions against Cuban officials and continued pressure on the island’s government.

Earlier this month, CIA Director John Ratcliffe also visited Havana in another unusual high level contact between the two countries.

Cuban officials have recently warned against any potential military confrontation, while the United States has continued to express concerns about security and stability in the region.

item.title: Britain plans tougher penalties to protect subsea internet cables
item.dc:creator: The Intel Desk
item.description: Britain has proposed stronger penalties for damage to subsea internet cables as it moves to strengthen protection of critical infrastructure.
item.content:encoded:
Britain has announced plans to introduce tougher penalties for damage to subsea internet cables as part of efforts to strengthen protection of critical national infrastructure.

The proposed measures would allow stronger action against shipowners and operators found responsible for reckless damage to undersea communication networks, including potential prison sentences in serious cases.

Subsea cables carry the vast majority of international internet traffic and support financial transactions, communications and digital services across the country.

Telecoms Minister Liz Lloyd said the government intends to update existing legislation and close legal gaps that could be exploited by hostile actors targeting critical infrastructure.

The plans follow growing concerns about threats to undersea cables and other strategic assets. British officials have previously raised concerns about Russian activities near critical infrastructure in and around UK waters.

The government said new measures would also address accidental cable damage caused by fishing activity and vessels dragging anchors, which remain among the most common causes of outages.

Officials are consulting industry stakeholders on the possibility of establishing a British flagged cable repair vessel and reviewing regulations to speed up the deployment of new subsea cables.

The proposed reforms form part of a broader effort to improve the resilience and security of the country’s communications infrastructure.

item.title: Trump weighs Iran ceasefire extension as negotiations continue
item.dc:creator: The Intel Desk
item.description: President Donald Trump says he will soon decide whether to approve a proposal to extend the ceasefire between the United States and Iran as negotiations continue.
item.content:encoded:
President Donald Trump said he will soon decide whether to approve a proposal to extend the ceasefire between the United States and Iran as negotiations continue.

The proposed agreement would extend the truce for an additional 60 days while both sides work toward a broader settlement of issues linked to the conflict.

Trump said any agreement would require unrestricted shipping through the Strait of Hormuz and reiterated that Iran must not develop a nuclear weapon.

Iranian officials indicated that discussions were progressing but said a final agreement had not yet been approved. Iranian media also reported differences between Washington and Tehran over key elements of the proposed arrangement.

The Strait of Hormuz remains a central issue in negotiations because of its importance to global oil and energy shipments.

Markets reacted positively to reports of possible progress, with oil prices easing as investors assessed the prospects of reduced tensions in the region.

Both countries continue to discuss broader demands, including sanctions, regional security concerns and future diplomatic arrangements.

item.title: Trump demands Strait of Hormuz reopen in possible Iran deal
item.dc:creator: The Intel Desk
item.description: Trump says he will soon decide on a possible deal to extend the Iran ceasefire, but Tehran disputes key parts of his account.
item.content:encoded:
US President Donald Trump said on Friday he would soon make a final decision on a possible deal to extend the ceasefire with Iran. After a two-hour meeting in the White House Situation Room, no decision had been reached.

In a post on Truth Social ahead of the meeting, Trump said Iran must agree never to have a nuclear weapon, and that the Strait of Hormuz must reopen immediately, toll-free and in both directions, to allow unrestricted shipping.

However, Iran disputed key parts of Trump’s post. Its Fars news agency, citing sources, reported that no clause on the toll-free reopening of Hormuz appeared in the text of the agreement, and called Trump’s claim about destroying Iran’s nuclear material “fundamentally baseless.”

Fars also said Tehran was demanding the release of $12 billion in frozen Iranian assets before negotiations could continue, and that the Strait of Hormuz would reopen only on Tehran’s terms, after the US lifts its blockade on Iranian ships. Iran’s foreign ministry spokesperson, Esmaeil Baghaei, told state TV there were currently no negotiations taking place on its nuclear programme.

The war, launched by the US and Israel on February 28, has killed thousands of people, mainly in Iran and Lebanon, and pushed energy prices higher as shipping through the Strait of Hormuz was disrupted. The proposed deal would extend an early-April ceasefire by 60 days to allow time for fuller negotiations. On Friday, oil prices fell and stocks rose on news of the potential agreement.

item.title: WHO reports 906 suspected Ebola cases as Bundibugyo outbreak continues
item.dc:creator: The Intel Desk
item.description: The World Health Organization says hundreds of suspected Ebola cases are under investigation as the Bundibugyo outbreak continues in the Democratic Republic of Congo and Uganda.
item.content:encoded:
The World Health Organization (WHO) says 906 suspected cases of Ebola are under investigation in the Democratic Republic of Congo as health authorities continue efforts to contain the Bundibugyo strain outbreak.

According to the WHO, 223 suspected deaths are also being investigated, while 125 cases and 17 deaths have been confirmed in affected areas of eastern Congo.

Uganda has also reported Ebola infections linked to the outbreak. The WHO said seven confirmed cases and one death had been recorded, although Ugandan health authorities later reported nine confirmed cases.

Health officials say no community transmission has been detected in Uganda.

The Bundibugyo strain of Ebola currently has no approved vaccine, making containment measures, testing and contact tracing critical to limiting further spread.

WHO officials estimate the fatality rate among confirmed cases could range between 30% and 50%, though investigations remain ongoing and early treatment can improve outcomes.

The agency said testing capacity is being expanded and additional suspected cases may be identified as surveillance efforts increase.

Health authorities have not yet determined whether the outbreak has reached its peak.

item.title: Ghana receives citizens evacuated from South Africa
item.dc:creator: The Intel Desk
item.description: Ghana has received its first group of citizens evacuated from South Africa following reports of anti immigrant tensions and attacks on foreign nationals.
item.content:encoded:
Ghana has received its first group of citizens evacuated from South Africa following reports of anti immigrant tensions and attacks on foreign nationals.

The flight, carrying around 300 Ghanaians, arrived in Accra where authorities provided reception support and initial reintegration assistance.

Officials said the evacuation was coordinated in response to safety concerns affecting foreign nationals in parts of South Africa.

Upon arrival, returnees were given transport assistance and guidance on reintegration support services.

item.title: Energy Ministry to launch real-time ECG command centre to monitor power distribution
item.dc:creator: The Intel Desk
item.description: Ghana’s Energy Ministry plans to establish a command-and-control centre to monitor Electricity Company of Ghana operations in real time and improve service delivery.
item.content:encoded:
Ghana’s Energy Minister Dr. John Abdulai Jinapor has announced plans to establish a dedicated command-and-control centre to monitor the operations of the Electricity Company of Ghana (ECG) in real time.

The initiative is aimed at improving accountability and enhancing the reliability of electricity distribution across the country.

The minister disclosed the plan during a meeting with ECG regional and district managers in the Ashanti Region.

The proposed system will allow the ministry to track transformers, monitor response times to outages, and assess overall service performance through a centralised digital platform.

As part of the reforms, ECG managers will be issued dedicated mobile devices linked to the system to improve communication between field teams and headquarters.

The ministry also plans to introduce a public short code for reporting power outages directly, as well as provide electric motorcycles and pickup trucks to improve field response times.

Dr. Jinapor said the initiative is intended to reduce delays in fault resolution and strengthen oversight of ECG operations nationwide.

The announcement forms part of broader reforms in the power sector, following recent interventions including procurement reviews and operational challenges within the distribution network.

item.title: GCB Bank named Best Regional Bank in West Africa at African Banker Awards
item.dc:creator: The Intel Desk
item.description: GCB Bank has been named Best Regional Bank in West Africa at the African Banker Awards held during the African Development Bank Annual Meetings.
item.content:encoded:
GCB Bank has been named Best Regional Bank in West Africa at the 20th African Banker Awards, held in Brazzaville on the sidelines of the African Development Bank Annual Meetings.

The award places Ghana’s largest locally owned bank among leading financial institutions recognised for performance across innovation, infrastructure financing, and inclusive banking initiatives.

The ceremony brought together senior African financial and political leaders, including representatives of Congo’s government and the President of the African Development Bank.

Ecobank was named Bank of the Year at the event, while other awards highlighted achievements in development finance, infrastructure investment, and gender-focused banking initiatives across the continent.

Organisers described the awards as a benchmark for excellence in African banking, recognising institutions driving growth and financial inclusion across multiple markets.

GCB Bank’s recognition adds to growing visibility for Ghana’s banking sector within regional financial systems.

item.title: Four illegal miners die after pit collapse at abandoned galamsey site in Ashanti Region
item.dc:creator: The Intel Desk
item.description: Four illegal miners have died after a collapsed pit at a former illegal mining site in the Amansie Central District of Ghana’s Ashanti Region.
item.content:encoded:
Four illegal miners have died after a pit collapsed at an abandoned illegal mining site in Skuumu near Gyaman-Nkwanta in the Amansie Central District of Ghana’s Ashanti Region.

The incident occurred around 4:00 pm on Wednesday, when the group reportedly entered the restricted site and the ground gave way, trapping them beneath the rubble.

Authorities say the site had previously been handed over to Logist Group Company Limited for environmental reclamation after it was declared exhausted by government officials.

Despite its closure and transfer for rehabilitation, the victims allegedly accessed the area without authorisation to continue illegal mining activities.

The leader of the group has been identified as Solomon Amadevor, while the identities of the other three victims have not yet been officially confirmed.

Police and emergency responders were deployed to the scene shortly after the collapse was reported, and an excavator was used to recover the bodies later the same evening.

The incident has renewed concerns over security at abandoned mining sites and the ongoing risks associated with illegal mining, locally known as galamsey, in Ghana.

item.title: Iceland’s parliament approves referendum on EU accession talks
item.dc:creator: The Intel Desk
item.description: Iceland’s parliament has voted to hold a referendum in August on restarting European Union accession talks, reviving debate over potential membership.
item.content:encoded:
Iceland’s parliament has voted in favour of holding a referendum on whether to restart European Union accession talks, setting up a nationwide vote scheduled for August 29.

The proposal forms part of a government-backed two-step plan that could eventually lead to EU membership if approved in a second referendum later in the decade.

Iceland previously abandoned EU negotiations in 2013 after a change in government, but renewed public debate has emerged due to rising living costs and geopolitical shifts following the war in Ukraine.

Under the plan, a “yes” vote in August would allow accession talks to resume, while a “no” vote would end the process entirely.

Lawmakers in the 63-seat parliament voted 34 in favour of the referendum, with eight against and several abstentions or absences.

Officials said the process is intended to give voters more clarity before any final decision on membership is made, given long-standing divisions in public opinion.

Icelandic leaders have previously suggested that fisheries and agriculture would be among the most complex issues in any potential negotiations with the EU.

item.title: US imposes fresh sanctions on Iran’s military-linked oil sales network
item.dc:creator: The Intel Desk
item.description: The United States has imposed new sanctions targeting vessels and entities involved in Iran’s oil trade, citing efforts to restrict military-linked revenue flows.
item.content:encoded:
The United States has imposed new sanctions targeting Iran’s military-linked oil trade, according to the US Treasury Department.

The measures include restrictions on eight vessels involved in transporting Iranian crude oil and petroleum products to global markets. Among the ships named were tankers flagged under the Marshall Islands, Comoros, and Panama.

The Treasury Department said the sanctions are aimed at limiting revenue streams used to support Iran’s military capabilities.

US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said the action was intended to prevent the Iranian government from using oil income to rebuild its armed forces.

The move comes amid broader tensions surrounding negotiations between Washington and Tehran over a tentative ceasefire extension and maritime access through the Strait of Hormuz.

Additional sanctions were also imposed on more than 15 entities across multiple jurisdictions, including firms based in Hong Kong and Dubai, accused of supporting Iran’s oil export infrastructure.

The US said some of the targeted entities were linked to Iran’s armed forces and were involved in facilitating petroleum sales through affiliated networks.

The developments highlight continued pressure on Iran’s energy sector despite ongoing diplomatic discussions.

item.title: US Immigration Agent Arrested Over Minneapolis Shooting Case
item.dc:creator: The Intel Desk
item.description: U.S. ICE agent Christian Castro was arrested in Texas and charged over the shooting of a Venezuelan man during a January incident in Minneapolis.
item.content:encoded:
A U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) agent has been arrested in Texas and charged in connection with the shooting of a Venezuelan man in Minneapolis earlier this year.

Christian Castro, 52, faces four counts of second degree assault and one count of falsely reporting a crime over the January 14 shooting of Julio Cesar Sosa-Celis, according to Minnesota prosecutors.

Investigators from Minnesota’s Bureau of Criminal Apprehension located Castro in Harlingen, Texas, where he was arrested on Friday with assistance from the Texas Rangers. He is expected to be transferred to Minnesota to face the charges.

Prosecutors allege Castro shot Sosa-Celis in the leg after firing through the front door of a house. Federal authorities later withdrew earlier claims that Sosa-Celis and two other men had attacked the agent, and the Justice Department subsequently dropped its case against the men.

The Department of Homeland Security criticised the prosecution, describing it as a political action and arguing that any alleged misconduct by the agent should be handled at the federal level.

The case is among several investigations involving federal immigration agents in Minneapolis following enforcement operations carried out earlier this year.

item.title: Myanmar president to visit India in first trip since taking office
item.dc:creator: The Intel Desk
item.description: Myanmar President Min Aung Hlaing is set to visit India for talks with Prime Minister Narendra Modi, marking his first official visit since taking office.
item.content:encoded:
Myanmar President Min Aung Hlaing is scheduled to visit India from May 30 to June 3, in what will be his first official trip to the country since assuming office, India’s foreign ministry said.

During the visit, he is expected to hold talks with Prime Minister Narendra Modi in New Delhi on June 1, according to the ministry statement.

The trip comes after Hlaing was elected president through a parliamentary vote on April 3, consolidating his political authority in Myanmar following the 2021 military takeover.

He had originally been scheduled to attend a regional summit in India, but the event was postponed, after which the visit was reclassified as an official bilateral trip.

The visit is expected to focus on diplomatic and regional cooperation issues, according to officials cited in the announcement.

item.title: Romanian prosecutors add hate speech charges against Andrew Tate in ongoing investigation
item.dc:creator: The Intel Desk
item.description: Romanian prosecutors have expanded their investigation into Andrew Tate by adding charges of inciting hatred and discrimination against women.
item.content:encoded:
Romanian prosecutors have expanded their ongoing criminal investigation into internet personality Andrew Tate by adding charges of inciting hatred and discrimination against women.

The Directorate for Investigating Organised Crime and Terrorism (DIICOT) said the new allegations relate to public statements made by Tate between 2021 and 2024, which prosecutors argue promoted hostility against women on social media.

Tate and his brother Tristan have been under investigation since 2022 on separate allegations, including human trafficking and forming an organised criminal group. Both have denied all wrongdoing.

Prosecutors said the case involves multiple suspects and includes accusations such as trafficking of minors, sexual exploitation, and money laundering, though none of the charges have yet gone to trial.

The brothers were previously placed under various levels of judicial restriction, including detention, house arrest, and later reduced supervision measures, which were lifted by a Romanian court in April.

Tate responded on social media platform X, denying wrongdoing and describing the case as a prosecution over speech, saying he would contest the allegations in court.

The investigation remains ongoing.

item.title: Greenpeace says illegal miners continue extracting billions in Amazon gold
item.dc:creator: The Intel Desk
item.description: Greenpeace says illegal gold mining continues across Brazil’s Amazon rainforest despite government efforts to crack down on the activity.
item.content:encoded:
Illegal miners are continuing to extract billions of dollars worth of gold from Brazil’s Amazon rainforest despite an ongoing government crackdown, according to a Greenpeace study.

The environmental group said miners have adapted to enforcement measures by using so called “ghost permits” to disguise the origin of illegally mined gold.

Greenpeace said its analysis found dozens of mining permits issued in areas that showed no visible mining activity, while large illegal operations continued near protected Indigenous territories and conservation zones.

Brazilian authorities have stepped up efforts against illegal mining in recent years, with federal police reporting major gold seizures during enforcement operations.

Indigenous leaders and environmental groups warned that illegal mining continues to damage forests, pollute rivers and threaten local communities across the Amazon region.

item.title: EU fines Temu €200m over illegal and unsafe products on platform
item.dc:creator: The Intel Desk
item.description: The European Union has fined Chinese-owned retailer Temu €200m after finding illegal and unsafe products on its platform, including faulty chargers and hazardous baby toys.
item.content:encoded:
The European Union has fined Chinese-owned online retailer Temu €200 million for allowing illegal and unsafe products to be sold on its platform.

The European Commission said Temu failed to properly identify and assess systemic risks linked to products sold to consumers, including dangerous baby toys and faulty electronic chargers.

The company has been under investigation since October 2024 over its compliance with obligations under EU law for designated Very Large Online Platforms.

An investigation that included mystery shopping tests found that a significant number of chargers purchased through the platform failed basic electrical safety standards. It also identified baby toys containing restricted chemicals or small detachable parts that posed safety risks.

Temu said it disagreed with the decision and described the fine as disproportionate, adding that it was reviewing its options and considering an appeal.

The company has been ordered to submit an action plan by 28 August to address the violations, after which the European Commission will assess whether compliance measures are sufficient.

EU tech commissioner Henna Virkkunen said the decision was intended to send a strong message regarding platform responsibility for consumer safety.

item.title: Taylor Swift files to trademark voice and image over AI concerns
item.dc:creator: The Intel Desk
item.description: Taylor Swift has filed trademark applications for her voice and image in an effort to protect against AI-generated impersonations.
item.content:encoded:
Taylor Swift has filed trademark applications in the United States to protect her voice and image amid growing concerns over artificial intelligence impersonations.

The filings include one application using a photo of Swift on stage during her Eras Tour, along with two audio clips of her introducing herself during album promotions.

The move follows increasing use of AI-generated content resembling public figures, including manipulated images and fake endorsements.

According to the filing details, the trademarks could allow Swift to challenge both direct copies and AI-generated imitations that are considered confusingly similar under US law.

item.title: Man jailed over planned attack on Taylor Swift Vienna concert
item.dc:creator: The Intel Desk
item.description: An Austrian court sentenced a 21-year-old man to 15 years in prison after he admitted planning a jihadist attack targeting Taylor Swift’s 2024 Vienna concerts.
item.content:encoded:
An Austrian court has sentenced a 21-year-old man to 15 years in prison after he admitted planning a jihadist attack targeting Taylor Swift’s Vienna concerts in August 2024.

The man, identified as Beran A under Austrian privacy laws, was convicted of multiple terrorism-related offences in Wiener Neustadt.

He was arrested after intelligence provided by the CIA alerted Austrian authorities shortly before the first of three scheduled concerts in Vienna.

All three shows were cancelled, affecting nearly 200,000 fans.

Authorities said the suspect had been radicalised and had pledged allegiance to the Islamic State group. Prosecutors said he attempted, but failed, to obtain weapons including a machine gun and a hand grenade.

A second man, named as Arda K from Slovakia, was also sentenced to 12 years in prison in connection with the case.

Court officials said Beran A expressed remorse during proceedings before the jury delivered its verdict.

item.title: Blue Origin rocket explodes during test in setback for New Glenn program
item.dc:creator: The Intel Desk
item.description: A Blue Origin New Glenn rocket exploded during a hot fire test, marking a major setback for the company’s efforts to compete with SpaceX.
item.content:encoded:
A Blue Origin New Glenn rocket exploded during a hot fire test on a launchpad in Florida, dealing a setback to the company’s space program.

Video from the scene showed the rocket igniting before erupting into a large fireball that sent flames and smoke into the air.

Blue Origin confirmed that an “anomaly” occurred during the test and said all personnel had been accounted for.

The company said the incident happened during a hot fire test, a procedure in which rocket engines are fired while the vehicle remains anchored to the ground.

Founder Jeff Bezos later said it was too early to determine the cause of the explosion.

The New Glenn rocket is central to Blue Origin’s efforts to compete with Elon Musk’s SpaceX in the commercial launch market and support Amazon’s satellite broadband ambitions.

item.title: Drone strike hits Romanian apartment building near Ukraine border, two injured
item.dc:creator: The Intel Desk
item.description: A drone struck a residential building in Galati, Romania, causing a fire and injuring two people near the Ukraine border.
item.content:encoded:
A drone struck a residential building in the city of Galati, Romania, near the Ukraine border, injuring two people, according to Romania’s emergency inspection authority.

Authorities said the drone hit a 10th floor apartment, causing a fire in the building.

Firefighters responded to the scene and brought the fire under control after evacuating around 70 residents.

The emergency services reported that the drone’s explosive payload detonated on impact, though no further technical details were provided.

In a separate incident, Romanian local authorities reported that another drone without an explosive charge was found in Maramures county, and the area was secured while investigations continued.

Authorities are also examining related drone activity reported across the border in Ukraine’s Odesa region, where drone attacks were recorded in the early hours of Friday.

item.title: Netanyahu orders expansion of Israeli control in Gaza
item.dc:creator: The Intel Desk
item.description: Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu said he had directed the military to expand Israeli control in Gaza to 70 percent as ceasefire negotiations with Hamas remain stalled.
item.content:encoded:
Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu said Thursday that he had instructed the military to expand Israeli control in Gaza to 70 percent of the territory as fighting and negotiations with Hamas continue.

Speaking at a conference in the occupied West Bank, Netanyahu said Israeli forces had already increased their control beyond previous positions and would continue operations step by step.

“We moved to sixty. First of all, seventy,” Netanyahu said while discussing military operations in Gaza.

Israeli officials describe the expanded areas as buffer zones intended to prevent future militant attacks following the Hamas assault on southern Israel in 2023.

The remarks come as ceasefire discussions backed by the United States remain deadlocked, with disagreements continuing over Hamas disarmament and Israeli troop withdrawals.

Palestinians and humanitarian groups have raised concerns that the expanding military zones could further displace civilians already confined to limited areas along the Gaza coast.

Health officials in Gaza said recent Israeli strikes killed multiple civilians, including children, during Eid al-Adha gatherings, while Israel said operations were targeting Hamas leaders and militant infrastructure.

Israel says Hamas militants continue to operate within Gaza, while Palestinian officials accuse Israel of widening military control under the cover of security operations.

item.title: Bolivia rules out president’s resignation amid growing unrest
item.dc:creator: The Intel Desk
item.description: Bolivia’s government rejected calls for President Rodrigo Paz to resign as protests and nationwide blockades continue to disrupt major cities and supply chains.
item.content:encoded:
Bolivia’s government on Thursday rejected calls for President Rodrigo Paz to resign as protests and transport blockades continue to spread across the country.

Minister of the Presidency Jose Luis Lupo described demands for the president to step down as “anti-democratic” and warned against attempts to undermine Bolivia’s elected institutions.

The unrest has disrupted supply chains and blocked major roads in several cities over the past month, increasing pressure on the government as economic frustrations grow.

President Paz has reportedly considered declaring a state of emergency that could allow troops to restore order and reopen transport routes.

Protesters, including labor groups and supporters of former President Evo Morales, have demanded that the government reverse austerity measures and address rising living costs.

Lupo said authorities were prioritizing dialogue but added that additional measures could be considered to maintain the movement of fuel, food and medical supplies.

The political crisis comes months after Paz entered office promising economic reforms and expanded foreign investment in sectors including mining, lithium and energy.

item.title: Three utility workers killed in drone strike in Russia controlled Donetsk region
item.dc:creator: The Intel Desk
item.description: A drone strike killed three utility workers in the Russia controlled Donetsk region of eastern Ukraine, according to local officials.
item.content:encoded:
A Ukrainian drone strike killed three utility workers in the Russia controlled part of the Donetsk region in eastern Ukraine, according to local authorities.

Officials said the workers were part of a repair crew for a water supply company and were travelling in a vehicle when it was struck.

A fourth person was seriously injured in the incident, according to the same reports.

Local officials also reported additional injuries in separate incidents across parts of the region under Russian control.

The area has remained a frequent site of fighting between Russian and Ukrainian forces since the start of the war.

item.title: US military denies claim aircraft was shot down near Iran’s Bushehr
item.dc:creator: The Intel Desk
item.description: The US military has denied Iranian state media claims that an American aircraft was shot down near Bushehr.
item.content:encoded:
The US military has denied claims that an American aircraft was shot down near Bushehr in Iran.

The denial follows a report from Iranian state television that said a US aircraft had been destroyed in the Jam governorate in Bushehr province, citing a local governor.

US military officials rejected the claim, stating that no US aircraft were shot down in the area.

No additional details were provided regarding the Iranian report or the circumstances surrounding the claim.

item.title: WHO identifies Ebola treatments and vaccines for trial use amid outbreak
item.dc:creator: The Intel Desk
item.description: The World Health Organization has identified experimental treatments and vaccines to be prioritized for clinical trials during an ongoing Ebola outbreak in Africa.
item.content:encoded:
The World Health Organization (WHO) has recommended prioritizing several experimental treatments and vaccines for clinical trials amid an ongoing Ebola outbreak in Africa.

The WHO identified three candidate treatments for the Bundibugyo strain of Ebola, including Mapp Biopharmaceutical’s MBP134, Regeneron’s maftivimab, and Gilead Sciences’ remdesivir.

The agency said these treatments, along with other vaccine candidates, should be evaluated in clinical trials to generate data on their effectiveness and safety.

Officials noted that there are currently no approved vaccines or therapies specifically for the Bundibugyo virus disease.

Among prevention options, several vaccine candidates are under consideration, including rVSV Bundibugyo and ChAdOx1 Bundibugyo, which are in different stages of development.

The WHO said it is working with health authorities and international partners to design and implement controlled clinical trials under ethical standards.

item.title: At least 52 killed in clashes between armed groups in Colombia
item.dc:creator: The Intel Desk
item.description: At least 52 fighters were killed in clashes between rival armed groups in a key cocaine trafficking region in Colombia, according to a rebel statement.
item.content:encoded:
At least 52 guerrilla fighters have been killed in clashes between rival armed groups in Colombia, according to a statement from one of the factions involved.

The fighting took place in a strategic region linked to cocaine production and trafficking, according to the group.

Colombian defense officials confirmed reports of armed clashes in the area and said troops had been deployed to protect civilians, but did not provide a confirmed death toll.

The violence occurred ahead of the country’s upcoming presidential election, as authorities continue to face challenges in controlling armed groups operating in rural regions.

The Colombian conflict, which has lasted for decades, remains closely tied to drug trafficking and illegal mining networks across multiple regions of the country.

item.title: US to quarantine Ebola-exposed citizens in Kenya under new containment plan
item.dc:creator: The Intel Desk
item.description: The US will isolate citizens exposed to Ebola in a facility in Kenya instead of returning them to the United States, according to White House officials.
item.content:encoded:
The United States is setting up a facility in Kenya to quarantine American citizens who have been exposed to Ebola, according to White House officials.

Under the plan, individuals who have been exposed but remain asymptomatic will be monitored at the facility in Kenya rather than being returned to the United States.

Officials said those who later develop symptoms will be transferred to medical facilities in third countries instead of being brought back to US soil.

The facility will be located at Laikipia Air Base in Nanyuki and is intended to support containment efforts for high risk individuals.

The US government said the strategy is aimed at preventing Ebola cases from entering the country while strengthening overseas monitoring and response capacity.

The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention has also introduced travel restrictions and screening measures for travellers coming from affected regions.

item.title: Cuba warns risk of US military action is increasing
item.dc:creator: The Intel Desk
item.description:
Cuba warned Thursday that the risk of military aggression from the United States is increasing as negotiations between Havana and Washington show little progress.

Deputy Foreign Minister Josefina Vidal accused the United States of attempting to portray Cuba as a national security threat in order to justify further pressure against the island.

“Every day the danger of military aggression against Cuba grows,” Vidal said during a legislative hearing at Cuba’s National Capitol.
item.content:encoded:
Cuba warned Thursday that the risk of military aggression from the United States is increasing as negotiations between Havana and Washington show little progress.

Deputy Foreign Minister Josefina Vidal accused the United States of attempting to portray Cuba as a national security threat in order to justify further pressure against the island.

“Every day the danger of military aggression against Cuba grows,” Vidal said during a legislative hearing at Cuba’s National Capitol.

The remarks come amid worsening tensions between the two countries following expanded US sanctions and growing disputes over fuel supplies, trade restrictions and diplomatic negotiations.

US Secretary of State Marco Rubio said earlier this week that discussions between Washington and Havana could still produce positive results, though Cuban officials questioned the seriousness of the negotiations.

Cuban authorities have repeatedly stated they will reject foreign interference in internal affairs while warning that additional escalation could further destabilize relations between the two countries.
